Annotating Data On Figures

Purpose

This notebook shows how to add labels and short annotations to a figure.

Annotation can be an effective way to highlight key findings in a figure, but it can also clutter a visualization with chartjunk.

Load Data

countries <- readr::read_csv("data/real/infant_mortality_countries.csv")
Rows: 105 Columns: 6
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (3): country_name, region, oil_exporting_country
dbl (2): income_usd_per_capita, infant_mortality_per_1000
lgl (1): oil_exporter

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Create or change columns mutate(); if_else() 
infant_mortality <- countries |>
  filter(!is.na(infant_mortality_per_1000)) |>
  mutate(
    country_label = stringr::str_replace_all(country_name, "\\.", " "),
    country_label = if_else(country_label == "Afganistan", "Afghanistan", country_label)
  )

Start With The Plot

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties alpha; size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
p1 <- ggplot(
  infant_mortality,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = region), alpha = 0.75, size = 2.6) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(70, 100, 300, 1000, 3000, 6000),
    labels = c("$70", "$100", "$300", "$1,000", "$3,000", "$6,000")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Income And Infant Mortality Across Countries",
    x = "Income per person (log-transformed)",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births",
    color = "Region"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p1

Label Outliers

Use geom_text() or ggrepel::geom_text_repel() when labels are attached to data points. The trick is to give the label layer a smaller dataset than the point layer.

Here, the first labeling rule is simple: label countries with infant mortality above 250 per 1,000 live births.

ggrepel uses random draws. You can use the seed property to make label placement predictable.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter() 
high_mortality_labels <- infant_mortality |>
  filter(infant_mortality_per_1000 > 250)
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings label
Geoms / stats geom_text_repel()
geom_text_repel() properties color; size 
p2 <- p1 +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
    data = high_mortality_labels,
    aes(label = country_label),
    color = "gray15",
    segment.color = NA,
    seed = 123,
    size = 3.5,
    min.segment.length = 0
  )

p2

This labels a few important points without asking the reader to read every country name.

Call Out One Value

Sometimes we want to call out one specific point. Here, we want the reader to notice the United States, so we add one label and draw one line to the point.

We use a line here, since there are too many points to just smack a label on the figure.

First make a one-row dataset for the point.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter() 
united_states <- infant_mortality |>
  filter(country_label == "United States")
p3 <- p2 +
  annotate(
    geom = "segment",
    x = 3800,
    xend = united_states$income_usd_per_capita,
    y = 80,
    yend = united_states$infant_mortality_per_1000,
    color = "gray20",
    linewidth = 0.4
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom = "text",
    x = 3700,
    y = 82,
    label = "United States",
    hjust = 1,
    color = "gray15",
    size = 3.3,
    fontface = "plain"
  )

p3

Add A One-Off Callout

Use annotate() to add brief commentary to a single point.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter() 
papua_new_guinea <- infant_mortality |>
  filter(country_label == "Papua New Guinea")
Plot component Code in this chunk
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties color; size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs() 
p2 +
  geom_point(
    data = papua_new_guinea,
    color = "firebrick",
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom = "curve",
    x = 1200,
    xend = papua_new_guinea$income_usd_per_capita,
    y = 95,
    yend = papua_new_guinea$infant_mortality_per_1000,
    curvature = 0.25,
    arrow = arrow(length = grid::unit(0.02, "npc")),
    color = "firebrick"
  ) +
  annotate(
    geom = "label",
    x = 1250,
    y = 105,
    label = "A country that is\ninteresting for\nsome reason.",
    hjust = 0,
    size = 3.4,
    fill = "white",
    color = "gray15"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Labels And Callouts Can Do Different Jobs",
    subtitle = "Labels identify selected outliers; the callout explains one point worth checking."
  )

Note how you have to set an x and y value. Getting these just right is a matter of trial and error.

Do Not Label Everything

It is possible to label every point. However, unless the figure has few points, this usually makes the figure unreadable.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; label
Geoms / stats geom_point(); geom_text_repel()
geom_text_repel() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_x_log10(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(
  infant_mortality,
  aes(x = income_usd_per_capita, y = infant_mortality_per_1000)
) +
  geom_point(color = "gray70", size = 2) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country_label),
    seed = 123,
    size = 2.6,
    max.overlaps = Inf
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(
    breaks = c(70, 100, 300, 1000, 3000, 6000),
    labels = c("$70", "$100", "$300", "$1,000", "$3,000", "$6,000")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Too Many Labels Compete With The Pattern",
    x = "Income per person",
    y = "Infant mortality per 1,000 live births"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

What To Annotate

Good annotations often mark:

  • a key value
  • a visible change
  • a benchmark or reference line
  • a group the reader should compare
  • a limitation or missing context

Takeaways

  • Annotation should direct attention, not clutter the plot.
  • Give label layers their own smaller datasets.
  • Use geom_text(), geom_label(), or ggrepel::geom_text_repel() for labels attached to data points.
  • Use annotate() for one-off callouts that are not rows in the data.
